title: Wildfire Prevention Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill requires federal agencies like the Forest Service to do more work to prevent wildfires on government land. They must cut down more trees, do more controlled burns, and use cattle grazing to reduce fire risks, with goals to increase these activities by 40% by 2029. The bill also creates a program to test new wildfire technology with private companies and makes it easier to do this work without long environmental reviews. Local governments and tribes can now join court cases about forest projects that help prevent fires or involve cutting timber.
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on Public Lands, Forests, and Mining. Hearings held.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Wildfire Prevention Act of 2025. The law has three main parts. The first part is called Accomplishments Over Rhetoric and includes rules about speeding up forest treatments, making reports each year, being more open about reducing dangerous forest materials, tracking carbon in forests by region, and measuring how well wildfire work is done. The second part is about Forest Management and covers managing plants around power lines, selling timber from national forests, removing dangerous trees more easily, giving local governments more say in lawsuits, and using cattle grazing to reduce wildfire risk. The third part is about Cultural Change in Agencies and requires using existing fast-track rules, creating partnerships to test new wildfire technology, and stopping certain old reports.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: Federal land means land in the National Forest System and public lands managed by the Bureau of Land Management. Hazardous fuels reduction activity means any work to manage plants and reduce wildfire risk, including cutting trees and doing controlled burns. It does not include giving out contracts for this work. National Forest System means the system of forests managed by the Forest Service, but does not include forest reserves that were not created from public domain land. Secretary concerned means the Secretary of Agriculture working through the Forest Service Chief for national forest land, or the Secretary of the Interior working through the Bureau of Land Management Director for other federal land. Wildland-urban interface means areas where homes and wildland plants meet, as defined in another law called the Healthy Forests Restoration Act of 2003.

sections.title: Section 101 Accelerating Treatments on Federal Land
sections.text: The responsible Secretary must figure out how many acres were treated each year from 2019 through 2023. This includes acres where trees were cut for commercial use, acres where trees were cut but not sold, and acres treated with controlled burns. They must calculate the average number of acres treated during those five years. Starting in 2025, the Secretary must set yearly goals for tree cutting and controlled burns. For 2025 and 2026, the goals must be at least as high as the 2019-2023 average. For 2027 and 2028, the goals must be at least twenty percent higher than that average. For 2029 and after, the goals must be at least forty percent higher than that average. Within ninety days of this law passing, and every year after, the Secretary must assign yearly acre amounts for tree cutting and controlled burns to different regions or states. The Secretary must make all this information available to the public on Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management websites. This section does not change other existing laws, including the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and the Wilderness Act. Setting these goals and assignments does not require environmental impact studies under the National Environmental Policy Act.

sections.title: Section 102 Annual Reports
sections.text: Starting by September 30, 2025, and every year after, the responsible Secretary must publish information on Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management websites about the previous year. The report must include how many acres were treated under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, how many acres had trees cut, how many acres were treated with controlled burns, and whether these numbers met or exceeded the yearly goals. The report must explain any problems or delays that stopped them from meeting goals, including lawsuits or delays in preparing environmental paperwork. The report must include how many acres had regeneration harvest, which means cutting trees so new ones can grow. For all these treated acres, the report must show how many were in areas where at least twenty-five percent of trees might die from insects and diseases over fifteen years, or in areas with very high or high wildfire danger. The report must show how many acres used fast-track environmental review processes like categorical exclusions or emergency actions. The report must show how many acres used partnerships with others to do the work, including good neighbor agreements, stewardship agreements, tribal forest protection agreements, or stewardship contracts.

sections.title: Section 103 Transparency in Hazardous Fuels Reduction Activity Reporting
sections.text: The responsible Secretary must include a special report in the materials sent to support the President's budget request to Congress. From 2025 through 2030, this report must show how many acres had dangerous fuel reduction work done each year using both the old way of counting and a new way. Starting in 2031, the report only needs to use the new way of counting. The new counting method must record each acre only once per year, even if multiple activities happened on that acre. The report must include which acres are in the wildland-urban interface, what the wildfire risk level was at the beginning and end of each year, what types of activities were done, how much the activities cost per acre, which region the acres are in, and how well the activities worked to reduce wildfire risk. The Secretary must make these reports available to the public on Department websites. Within ninety days of this law passing, the Secretary must create standard procedures for tracking data about dangerous fuel reduction activities. These procedures must include regular reviews to make sure data is accurate and entered on time, methods to check that data matches the actual work done, analysis of how well the activities work in the short and long term, and ways to tell which acres are inside or outside the wildland-urban interface. Within fourteen days of creating these procedures, the Secretary must send Congress a report describing the procedures and recommendations for improving data tracking.

sections.title: Section 104 Regional Forest Carbon Accounting
sections.text: Starting by September 30, 2025, and every three years after, the Secretary of Agriculture working through the Forest Service Chief must figure out the net forest carbon balance for each Forest Service region. This means determining whether the National Forest System land in each region takes in more carbon from the air than it releases, making it a carbon sink, or releases more carbon than it takes in, making it a carbon source. The Secretary must use data from the forest inventory and analysis program to make these calculations. The Secretary must publish this information on the Forest Service website.

sections.title: Section 105 Wildland Fire Performance Metrics
sections.text: Within eighteen months of this law passing, the responsible Secretary must send a report to specific Congressional committees about existing ways to measure performance and potential new ways to measure results in reducing wildfire risk on federal land. The report must identify solutions to track how well dangerous fuel reduction activities and forest restoration treatments work. This includes strategies to track whether land management activities are reducing wildfire dangers and ways to count and track acres that are being maintained, to track local outcomes, to create standard national monitoring measures, to measure how much catastrophic wildfire risk is reduced, to identify problems with models and data that prevent yearly wildfire risk mapping updates, and to use advanced technologies that will improve the quality of reported information. The Congressional committees that will receive this report are the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources, the Senate Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry, the House Committee on Natural Resources, and the House Committee on Agriculture.

sections.title: Section 201 Vegetation Management, Facility Inspection, and Operation and Maintenance Relating to Electric Transmission and Distribution Facility Rights-of-Way
sections.text: This section changes an existing law about managing dangerous trees near electric power lines. It increases the distance from ten feet to fifty feet around electric power lines where dangerous trees can be removed. The section also adds new rules for special use permits and easements given to electric power line owners and operators on National Forest System or Bureau of Land Management land. The responsible Secretary can give permission to cut and remove trees or other plants near electric facilities without requiring a separate timber sale, as long as the cutting follows the applicable plans and environmental laws. If the power line owner or operator sells any of the removed material, they must give the proceeds from the sale to the responsible Secretary, minus transportation costs. However, the power line owner or operator is not required to sell the removed material.

sections.title: Section 202 Timber Sales on National Forest System Land
sections.text: This section changes the dollar amount for timber sales on National Forest System land. It increases the threshold from ten thousand dollars to fifty-five thousand dollars. This change affects when certain procedures must be followed for timber sales under the National Forest Management Act of 1976.

sections.title: Section 203 Categorical Exclusion for High-Priority Hazard Trees
sections.text: A high-priority hazard tree is a standing tree that poses a visible danger to people or federal property because of damage to its roots, trunk, stem, or limbs, or because of how it is leaning. The Secretary must determine that the tree is very likely to fall and would likely cause injury or damage if it fell. The tree must be within three hundred feet of a National Forest System road with maintenance level 3, 4, or 5, along a National Forest System trail, or in a developed recreation site operated by the Secretary. High-priority hazard tree activity means forest management work to reduce risks from these trees, including pruning, cutting down, and disposing of the trees. This does not include activities in wilderness areas or wilderness study areas, building permanent roads or trails, activities on land where Congress or Presidential order restricts removing vegetation, activities inconsistent with land management plans, or activities in inventoried roadless areas. Within one year of this law passing, the Secretary of Agriculture must create a categorical exclusion for high-priority hazard tree activities. A categorical exclusion is a type of fast-track environmental review under the National Environmental Policy Act. The Secretary must follow environmental laws and use extraordinary circumstances procedures when deciding whether to use this categorical exclusion. Projects using this categorical exclusion cannot exceed three thousand acres.

sections.title: Section 204 Intervenor Status
sections.text: For lawsuits about qualified projects, local government units or Indian Tribes have the right to join the lawsuit if the project is on federal land next to their land or has enough connection to their land, as determined by the responsible Secretary. The project must be approved by the responsible Secretary and must either reduce wildfire, insect, or disease risk, or generate money from harvesting timber. If the local government or Indian Tribe joins the lawsuit, they are considered full participants in any settlement talks about the project.

sections.title: Section 205 Utilizing Grazing for Wildfire Risk Reduction
sections.text: Within eighteen months of this law passing, the responsible Secretary must create and send to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources and the House Committee on Natural Resources a strategy to analyze and find opportunities to use livestock grazing as a wildfire risk reduction tool on federal land. The strategy must follow laws that apply to the responsible Secretary. The strategy must analyze opportunities to increase use of livestock grazing authorities including changes to grazing permits or leases to allow differences, to use targeted grazing to reduce dangerous fuels, to use advanced technologies to adjust where livestock are placed, to use livestock grazing to get rid of invasive annual grasses and as a strategy for restoration and recovery after fires, and to make it easier and faster to temporarily use vacant allotments during extreme weather events or natural disasters. The strategy must also analyze any other opportunities the responsible Secretary thinks are appropriate. This section does not affect any livestock grazing programs carried out by the responsible Secretary as of when this law passes, or any legal authority for those programs.

sections.title: Section 301 Mandatory Use of Existing Authorities
sections.text: Within three years of this law passing, the responsible Secretary must use at least one fast-track environmental review authority for each unit of federal land that contains land with high wildfire risk or high risk from insects and diseases. The Secretary can choose from six different existing authorities created by previous laws, including sections from the Healthy Forests Restoration Act of 2003, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, and the Wildfire Suppression Funding and Forest Management Activities Act. These authorities allow faster environmental reviews for certain types of forest management projects.

sections.title: Section 302 Public-Private Wildfire Technology Deployment and Testbed Partnership
sections.text: This section creates definitions for key terms and establishes a pilot program. Appropriate committees include several Senate and House committees dealing with agriculture, natural resources, energy, commerce, science, and transportation. Covered agencies include federal land management agencies, the Department of Defense, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, the United States Fire Administration, the Federal Emergency Management Agency, the National Aeronautics and Space Administration, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, and other federal agencies involved in wildfire response. Covered entities include private companies, nonprofit organizations, and colleges and universities. Within sixty days of this law passing, the Secretary of Agriculture and Secretary of the Interior working together, coordinating with the heads of covered agencies, must establish a pilot program for testing new wildfire prevention, detection, communication, and mitigation technologies. The program must be incorporated into an existing interagency group on wildfires, identify key technology priority areas including dangerous fuels reduction, dispatch communications, remote sensing and tracking, safety equipment, and operational dashboards, and connect selected entities with the right agencies to test technology during real wildfire work and training. To participate, entities must submit applications with proposals to test technologies in the priority areas. The Secretaries must give priority to entities developing emerging technologies like artificial intelligence, quantum sensing, computing applications, augmented reality, and 5G networks for wildfire work. The Secretaries must make priority areas public and invite entities to apply. Each year for the duration of the program, the Secretaries must send reports to the appropriate committees listing participating entities, describing tested technologies, estimating costs of acquiring and scaling up technologies, describing outreach efforts, and making assessments and recommendations about new technologies for federal wildfire efforts. The pilot program ends seven years after this law passes.

sections.title: Section 303 Repeal of FLAME Reports
sections.text: This section changes the FLAME Act of 2009 by removing the requirement for certain reports and renumbering the remaining sections accordingly.
